serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of stir-fried kang kong with sambal, often served as a side dish, is approximately 180 grams.

nutrient_source.title: Kangkung Goreng Belacan
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values adapted from 'Kangkung Goreng Belacan' (stir-fried water spinach with shrimp paste) from IMR Malaysia MyFCD 2017. Sambal and belacan are both chili-shrimp paste condiments used in the same preparation style, yielding a closely comparable nutrient profile for the vegetable base and cooking method.

gi_source.title: Analogous to leafy green vegetables (spinach and related species)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry for kang kong or water spinach found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 15 by analogy with other non-starchy leafy green vegetables such as spinach (GI 15), which share similar low-carbohydrate, high-fibre characteristics. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (6.5 g carbohydrate per 100g × 1.8 serving factor) / 100 ≈ 1.8.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from sambal and belacan seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should consume this dish in moderation due to its elevated sodium content (approximately 450 mg per 100 g) from sambal paste and shrimp paste, and should request reduced-seasoning preparations where possible.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and low net carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With an estimated glycemic index of 15 and only 6.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g, this dish is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed as part of a balanced meal, though sodium intake should still be monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium and saturated fat content from cooking oil and sambal
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While the vegetable base provides dietary fibre beneficial for cardiovascular health, the sodium content and saturated fat from cooking oil and sambal warrant moderation for individuals with cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-fried Kang Kong with Sambal is a widely consumed Southeast Asian vegetable dish, particularly popular in Malaysian and Singaporean cuisine. The dish features water spinach (Ipomoea aquatica), known locally as kang kong or kangkung, which is stir-fried rapidly at high heat in a wok with a spicy sambal paste. The sambal is typically made from dried or fresh chili peppers, garlic, shallots, and belacan (fermented shrimp paste), producing a pungent, savory, and fiery flavour profile. The high-heat wok cooking preserves a slight crunch in the hollow stems and tender leaves of the kang kong, contrasting with the rich, aromatic sauce. This dish is commonly served as a side dish alongside steamed rice, grilled fish, or other zi char (Chinese-style cook-to-order) dishes. Nutritionally, it provides dietary fibre (approximately 2.2 g per 100 g) and moderate protein (2.5 g per 100 g), but its sodium content (approximately 450 mg per 100 g) is a consideration for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Its low glycemic index makes it suitable for blood sugar management.
